message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Assessing the impact of temperature on COVID-19 epidemiology is critical for implementing non-pharmaceutical interventions. However, few studies have accounted for the nature of contagious diseases, i.e., their dependent happenings.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Aim</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We aimed to quantify the impact of temperature on the transmissibility and virulence of COVID-19 in Tokyo, Japan. We employed two epidemiological measurements of transmissibility and severity: the effective reproduction number (
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    ) and case fatality risk (CFR).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We used empirical surveillance data and meteorological data in Tokyo to estimate the
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    and time-delay adjusted CFR and to subsequently assess the nonlinear and delay effect of temperature on
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    and time-delay adjusted CFR.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    For
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    at low temperatures, the cumulative relative risk (RR) at first temperature percentile (3.3°C) was 1.3 (95% confidence interval (CI): 1.1-1.7). As for the virulence to humans, moderate cold temperatures were associated with higher CFR, and CFR also increased as the temperature rose. The cumulative RR at the 10
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    and 99
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    percentiles of temperature (5.8°C and 30.8°C) for CFR were 3.5 (95%CI: 1.3-10) and 6.4 (95%CI: 4.1-10.1).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    This study provided information on the effects of temperature on the COVID-19 epidemiology using
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    and time-delay adjusted CFR. Our results suggest the importance to take precautions to avoid infection in both cold and warm seasons to avoid severe cases of COVID-19. The results and proposed framework will also help in assessing possible seasonal course of COVID-19 in the future.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: The impact of temperature on the transmission potential and virulence of COVID-19 in Tokyo, Japan
